---
title: "ANOVA Table"
description: "Partition regression variation and run F-tests with an ANOVA table."
categories: [Regression, "Inference & Prediction"]
---

::: {.callout-note title="Introduction"}
Use the ANOVA table to partition sums of squares and F-test whether the
predictors collectively improve on an intercept-only model.
:::

```{r}

# read data
gpa <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR03.txt", header=TRUE)

# fit linear model (defined when checking assumptions on this data)
gfit <- lm(Y ~ X, data=gpa)

# anova table
anova(gfit)

# f-test for value of variables in model (H_0: intercept-only model)
f <- qf(.95, 1, 118) #f*
MSR <- 3.588
MSE <- 0.388
Fstar <- MSR/MSE #f statistic

# calculate r-squared
SSR <- 3.588
SSE <- 45.818
SSTO <- SSR+SSE
Rsquared <- SSR/SSTO

# not sure????
conc <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR15.txt", header=TRUE)
gfit_conc <- lm(conc$Concentration ~ conc$Hours)
gfit_conc_full <- lm(conc$Concentration ~ 0 + factor(conc$Hours))
anova(gfit_conc, gfit_conc_full)
compare <- qf(.975, 3, 10)
f_star <- (2.7673/3)/(.1574/10)
```

## Answers

### F Test
Null Hypothesis: $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$

Alternate Hypothesis: $H_a: \beta_1 \neq 0$

Because the test statistic is larger than F\*, I can reject the null hypothesis
with 95% confidence. I conclude that the full regression model is better than
an intercept-only model.

### Unknown Test????
$H_0: \hat{Y} = -0.3240X + 2.5753$; $H_a: \hat{Y} \neq -0.3240X + 2.5753$

This information does not indicate what regression function is appropriate,
since all other regression functions are included in the alternate hypothesis.
If we want to explore alternate regression functions, we can look to other
diagnostics and examine the residuals.


## See also

- [Simple Linear Regression](simple-linear-regression.qmd)
- [Check Assumptions](check-assumptions.qmd)

*Data: `CH03PR03.txt`, `CH03PR15.txt` — see [sources & provenance](../../data/README.md#regression).*
